speaker: Rob Moore
words: On this tape, Marvin Long goes into more detail on some of the points touched on in the first interview. These include operation of the brick factory where the bricks were made that rebuilt Kendrick, the Kendrick fire, the grain tramway, ice cutting in the winter, and the move that Long's made down to Kendrick in Cedar Creek country.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were you here when the Kendrick fire, the 1904 fire happened in Kendrick?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, I was living up there on the Potlatch Ridge and we saw the smoke coming out of the canyon and my brother and I had...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You had to be there during the daytime?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: My brother and I the next day had to come down to Kendrick to see the ruins and it was still smoldering the whole canyon. Everything was burnt. All the business districts were burnt out. There wasn't a place to unify those bricks. Every business.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Is this being taped?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, and that night, that day, the next day, every nordian was passed. Every building on Main Street had to be fireproof, which is the reason now that Main Street is still fireproof.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they ever find out how that fire started?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, not exactly. Anyway, it was on about noon. It was a middle day, an oddest time of summer.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I'm just not sure. They didn't know how it started. I've got the wrong story, but I'm sorry. Did anybody have their idea about how the... I don't know. There was so much... No, there were so many fires them days, there was so much trash around them. They didn't keep things cleaned up like they did now. It could have started the most... You know, they could have been... It could have been combustion or it could have been sent by... See, that's what it's done. They rolled around. But there never any answer for it, I remember.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: It had to have been burned twice, you know?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Or at least it was. It had fire sure at a different time, but that was the only time I remember of it burning down entirely. There'd been a lot of... I mean the business part of it. Prior to that, it was not the last.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did it begin as a residential section?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, they didn't get any trash. Well, there might have been one or two. No, these are old buildings down here, if you look at the construction. And... Well, I guess through the ice.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: And you can tell on the other side where the park is there. There's one brick new house being built there. And yeah, there's the old connection house. It's the old building house.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: You're good.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I guess on that side of the street, it must have burnt down further because there was some brick.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: On the south side of the street?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: And it passed the ordinance... That night it was passed the city council. That, I remember, was the fire.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did they find that fire?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Didn't find it. It burnt. Didn't save anything. Yeah, well, they got some stuff out. A lot of the stuff they hold out in the union was burnt. It just burnt. It just took everything. It didn't.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there a fire coming in here? Like a hunkerway?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Just a water brigade, I imagine. Oh, there wasn't any water. Well, some cisterns. There was no water. Nothing to fight the fire with. Didn't like our reservoir there, no. There was tremendous pressure here. All kinds of water.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I imagine some of these guys were...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: What do you call them? Oat and wagons?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: They'd like to have some of our pressure some time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, so I guess after that, that brick mill that was here was pretty busy then, wasn't it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, they couldn't keep up with the orders and the white brick that's here in town was shifting. I told you that. It was shifting before it came. But they... It was so long before it got down to all the time. It produced here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How does the brick mill work? The brick factory?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Oh...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Got you out of watch in enough time?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, they got the clay down up there on the hill. I can tell you where it picked up and it was shooting. They just dumped it in the chills. And it went down to the brickyard. And then it was mixed. I don't know if it was water. And it was pressed.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, how close? They had a big oven, too?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, they had a kiln where they had this fabric. But I don't think they needed the fabric. They shipped them in. These white brick here is what you call them. Well, that's what's going on where their life's going. I think all they ever moved here was these red bricks.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't they make the old brick here? Which? The red brick?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Well, I mean, they must have made here. These buildings that's made out of different colored brick downtown, which is down there in Duke looking City. The main one is this Paternal Temple building. They couldn't get enough brick here, so they shipped them in from Spokane on freight. And that's where we got the two colors of brick.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: See them white colored?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: We shipped them from Spokane. And all these red brick were local, made up here. You know, they couldn't make them. Fast enough.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, the clay was dug out of a dry pit?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, it was a pit like this. I'm sure you'd have heard of it coming from here on the hill.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Then it was dumped in. See, when thinking about it, it was usually a... It wouldn't have been a brick there. It wouldn't have been a brick there if there wasn't clay. Yeah, it wouldn't have been there.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Usually a clay pit has a big... It's a big... Long time series, like a big water hole. And they use a Fresno to scoop the clay off the side. It can work out that way.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was it done?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: They scooped this.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: They did it with scoops. They had horses then. And they did it the same way, but they put it in this chute and shot it down. They set a hole in it. They shot it down to the brick there where they pressed it. And they mixed it down there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When it was shooting down, was it powder?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, it was just clay.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like rocks rolling down the clay?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: It was clay.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was it wet then?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, it was just like from out on the ground. It was wet afterwards. That's pretty... Well, that's pretty... 13, 14 years old. I don't even know about it, but I can't.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take them to do the segment? Build it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, it was all too fast. They started down. Of course, they were drilling here for a year. It didn't matter. It was one of those parts that was fast.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, like I said, you couldn't get... There's no place like me and my brother.

words: How do people usually shop and get where they live?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: What they do. Well, they make you friends. I guess they've done the best they could. I don't know. Well, the whole town wasn't grown up. Families were all here and they asked why. They got them in. They were doing business here about the next day and dancing and everything else. Just to bear to, they were doing business down there. Coming down here, there's still some more. There's some of them doing business here. Coming down.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Just by putting up tents in the old locations? Did they build a separate little tent town?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, yeah. It was first, you know, until they got something built.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I guess. I don't remember too much of anything from that. But I know they've done business in tents here. Until they got something.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: You ever see?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: They had it. The buildings went up all the fashion.

words: Okay.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That idea of the way the clay came down the chute, reminded me of that tram.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Told me that tram, the train comes and slashes on these carriers. And these pressure, the gravity, of course, is what took it down. They had too much gravity. They had to break up the top. It was nothing to go back, see, if they could.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how'd they get it back up again?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: They pushed. The carrier.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: It'd go back up. It'd carry itself. It'd be empty, see. The weight coming down would bring it back up, go around. And they had to break this going down some years. Them days they had to handbrake it. Handbrakes. The man had to be right there all the time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were the breaks? Was it like a big drone? Like a big drone?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I imagine. Well, yeah. I imagine. You know, you forget. I was just a kid. Right. But that's how they did it. I could see them there raking. When it was coming down, when they was loading it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was the brakeman on top or on bottom?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: He was on top.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: This was the bottom. They was taking them off. Uh -huh.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How long did it take them to unload a car? Would they pause right away?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, they didn't move a car. The sacks just kept coming. They had to keep that running until they run out. Oh. They could stop at night. They just grabbed the proposition. Uh -huh. They could run just as long as there was sacks down there. Well, how far apart were the carriers on the... I don't know. Maybe.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I couldn't tell. About 50 feet, maybe. Maybe not that much.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You had any idea?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, I never saw it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is that a picture show? What? Is that a picture on that truck show?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: It's another show.

words: There must be one sack after another coming down?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. They put it on, now you break it up. Now, if they'd had... If they wanted to take... Sometimes they would take feet and see the back. And if it weighed as much, you see, they didn't have to break so much because... They weighed it. Well, for one sack, it would take one sack back up. Right. But it wouldn't.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Wouldn't that work?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, yeah. That's the way they set stuff back up on top. These farmers don't do stuff like that. They set it back up.

speaker: Rob Moore

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. It would break up at the top. There's a gravity deal.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And how long did that thing work for? How long was that like? How long were you using it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, I don't know. I could tell you when it started to go out. It would go out. It would go out to see the horse and buggy business. Mm -hmm. When cars come in, trucks come in. They'd have a way to try and...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And when they got to the bottom of the tramp, they had one guy there loading or a couple guys unloading? How many of them?

speaker: F. Marvin Long

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Maybe the guy had trucks on top that would break. They'd have them trucked to it. Right. Because he couldn't...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And when the guy at the bottom took it off, where did it go from there?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: It went to the piles. Mm -hmm. So the separate buggy scene is shot down at the warehouse with the fucking piles. Hand -trucked. Oh, hand -trucked. I don't know.

words: Did you ever go for a ride when you were killing that train?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I never... Was there any... I never wanted to. Nobody else did. Huh. Mm -hmm. Pretty risky deal, if you're willing to show me. You can't show me. You can't. If you want to go, you better get off. Mm -hmm. I don't think we're in there for long. I never will. Right.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Do you know if that...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: This guy's dead. Um... This guy's dead. There he is. It's a true damn super -true. I don't know what that thing was.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Can you, uh... You were telling me before about, um... When you had the meat market and your ice cutting operation.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, that's... The story about that is... Every year we were in business. When we put up ice off the creek. And it was only during that time... It was only one winter that we had to ship our ice in. Out of all the years. And afterwards, we never wondered if we could cut ice after refrigeration come in. It just... It seems like a... It don't seem...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is it a coincidence, or a lack of nature, or what?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I don't know. I'll figure it out. I don't know. I see.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: But isn't that quite a coincidence? To hook up?

words: Well, how many years did you cut ice out of there?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well... We never started cutting ice down here until like in 19... ...19 -8.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, one wasn't... But the other one wasn't.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I was going to tell you. There was big holes on these... You know, on Cut Latch Creek here. And that's what... Now it's filled up the rock. There's one over here we call the Old Feminine. Over here by the warehouse. Back there against the wall. And that's where we used to get our ice. And I think we could all, during that time... ...there was just that one winter we had shipping ice from Spokane.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Not Chish at night. It was one of them lakes up there. From... ...Cocaine. Coca -Cola. Coca -Cola.

words: Did you go out in the winter and saw blocks off? What happened? How did the work go? How did it go?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: You saw this when it was freezing weather... ...and you put it in the ice house. In one big... ...put the cakes up. You know, these cake size. And we were packed in there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: But how big were these cakes?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, they were... 80 to 100 pounds. Something like that. Some big ones, you know. Some of the house picked the ice one. Some of them got pretty heavy. And they would... ...solid pack this ice... ...and all around the edges. There'd be... ...a place about that thick... ...in the wall. That kept the air out.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About two feet thick?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: We saw this.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: About two feet thick?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah, I don't remember if it was about half too deep down. I don't remember. But that's what kept the ice from all melting. It melted. There was an awful... ...percentage of waste. A lot of shrinkage in it. And the ice... ...you know, that no matter how... ...wanna be packed in and out. Yeah, it opened up after that. I don't know what ever day to get your ice up, Jim. But it got so much louder. It put the ice up the most. That's the ice you'd carry.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How much ice did you have to cut for one day?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I don't know. I usually estimate it in. It is probably... ...I don't know.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: We're quite a stack.

words: How big a stack was that?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: It was a little bit of a jolty, I don't know. I don't know. I don't know. Just... ...it's gonna be... ...three... ...three or four pieces. I got it.

words: Did you go out and cut ice with your father for a bit?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No. We had a crew of men. I didn't. I was farming out. I was like two men. At the service. Boy, I was... ...there was four of us boys then. And I was... ...I was farming out. At 19. I had farmed me out in 1910. And I come back into business in 1920. I was working for the other guy on the hot plant. Instead of calling me the ocean head, I was more than draggers. General merchandise.

words: Well, not in Canada. I worked down in Oregon. I still worked. 20 guys working in Oregon.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Henryville. Well, before I went down there, I used to work for the old draggers. My father named Fred Beckwith, and then I worked a year for him. He sold out. The Henry Store Company. And I worked for them.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: How long was I working for?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: A couple of years. I went to school.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: You're not?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: And then I worked all the time in school.

words: That was kind of great, wasn't it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. For that, I was working first all night, worked Saturday and Sunday. Tried to do a full -time job.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Afternoon. Noon. 4 o 'clock. It was first 3.

words: How about the trip down before the

words: Crescent? The Crescent Deliva? Do you remember anything about that?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I was telling you, I remembered all I remember is when I I was had all the furniture in this wagon. There were supplies for a little bit. Trunks and boxes and stuff. Tom, my older brother, was sitting on a trunk on the back end of the wagon holding me. And all I remember that trip was all coming down there, buddy.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: All I remember is when we landed down there this blameless old crippled guy and his mother, you know, blue, all gray. And that's all I remember. That's the first thing I remember. I don't remember one thing that went on out on on the senior creek.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Isn't that funny? Was that quite a difference for you?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Moving from the daylight to dark.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: I was in the timber. I hadn't seen daylight yet. So when I got out in these open spaces, and of course, first job I met, and that's quite an impression.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Um.

words: Oh, you were saying also that when you were about 11 and 12, you you used to have a job of buckling skates?

words: Buckling skates, yeah. This skating rink. I told you about that.

words: Well, how did it get?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, they had this skating rink. And then you had to pay so much. I don't know what, anyway. My brother and I, we buckled skates to get free skating. That's the pay we got.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, we had it work in the evening. It went about an hour, I imagine.

words: Yeah. I don't know. It took like 25. Or you get skateboard if you wanted to. You know, you had to exercise.

words: Well, some of them did and some didn't. I don't know if they had to work.

words: What they called those?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Didn't need any much music, no. There's a lot of noise just in there. I don't think we had music.

words: These were covered over there? What? These were recorded inside?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Aunt had a lot of chickens and geese and ducks and cool things. And I had to help take care of them to start off with. And after that, I looked after them myself until I got home again. And she was afraid I'd get lost. So I says, I don't want to get lost. I says, I've known these woods ever since I was born. But now I don't know what you expect me to get lost. Because I'm not very easily turned around if I do say so. If I don't come out one place, I can get my money close to it. Especially if there's an old barn mop in Vancouver. And so I went on a mouthful of picking. And this year, I think I got 41 gallons out of that place. And so, practically all of them. A dollar a gallon.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: A dollar a gallon?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Well, it was worth that. Or if it was worth anything, I'd have to get bent over all day in the house and pick and bury. I thought to myself, they could give me what I had for them or they could go without. And once more, I wasn't going to tell them where my patch was. I says, that's my secret. They didn't tell me then until I got all the birds on. After I had, I told them where it was. Even I even took Mrs. Ham one day during the last afternoon. But I expected the badger told her she'd meet me down on the meadow. Bob Clines meadow, as they called it. And I can't be sure where the huckleberries are. And she'd be like, please maybe we'll shoot a piece of that. Oh, I don't want to.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Wasn't that selfish?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: But I worked hard for those birds and I intended to have them.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did you do your own sewing those days?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Yeah. Our threads in my hands were kind of, I used to do quite a bit of hand sewing too. But, I think it was because they're kind of better now.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did you have a big garden?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Pretty good size.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Could you raise everything up there?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: That's a little bad to freeze up there. Well, we'd have to kind of look out and come along real nice maybe for a week or so. And the next thing you know you had a big frost and your garden looked kind of limp. But it got along pretty well. Pretty good garden. It's now 1973 and Larry French told me, night before last, that they had a frost and it got down to 30. I don't know how it got down to it, but they had a frost. It froze little ice. That is frosty ice. And this is July 8th.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Isn't that something to have?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: So that would be July 5th. They had it down to 30. You wouldn't expect anything from this part of the country.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did you have fruit trees?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Well, we had a few apple trees.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: And what kind of apples could you grow?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Just an ordinary apple.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Summer transparents or winter apples?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Well, we had one brand that was winter apples. But it didn't do so well because generally about the time they should be getting ripe and nice and they won't come across, and we wouldn't have any apples. It's not anything. Once in a while we got a good layoff.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever have trouble up there with bears in your apple trees or bears in your chicken? Or other animals in your chicken?

speaker: Marsha Long
words: We'd see a bear now and then wander around.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: We left them alone. They left us alone for a little while. We had pretty good dogs. The dogs didn't particularly like the bear, but they'd bark and that would give the bear warmth. They'd better get out of the way.

speaker: Marsha Long
words: Did you know that this professor, Murman, who was in Moscow School, my mother taught him one year under him, he says, I want you to take that seventh grade. She says, well, I don't like teaching down. She says, I want you to do this time whether you like it or not. I was a slow girl in there and I was bound to make her learn how to spell if it's possible. How to spell and leave off making spells. He says, sure, I don't want to teach you. I know how to do it. He says, maybe I can do it, but I don't like the job.
